Facts.text: Louis V becomes the sole King of West Francia and undisputed king of the Franks after the death of his father Lothair
Dirk VI becomes count of Holland
Assassination of Charles the Good, Count of Flanders
Hussite George of Poděbrady chosen as the 16th King of Bohemia
Great Fire of Meireki destroys 60-70% of the Japanese capital city of Edo, killing an estimated 100,000 people
French Canadian explorer Pierre Le Moyne d’Iberville is the first European to discover the mouth of the Mississippi river
The Loves of Mars and Venus becomes the first ballet performed in England
American troops begin shelling the British in Boston
Pennsylvania ends prohibition of theatrical performances
Long-distance communication speeds up with the unveiling of a semaphore machine in Paris
Congress standardizes US weights & measures
US Congress bans the slave trade within the US, effective January 1, 1808
The inaugural meeting of the Wernerian Natural History Society, a former Scottish learned society, was held in Edinburgh.
Signing of Kandyan treaty by British invaders and Sri Lankan King
1st Evangelical church building dedicated, New Berlin, Pennsylvania
Territory of Arkansas organized
US passed its 1st immigration law
Interstate commerce comes under federal control
1st grand opera in US sung in English, NYC
New England Asylum for the Blind, 1st in US, incorporated, Boston
John Frazee becomes 1st US sculptor to receive a federal commission
Territory of Washington organized after separating from Oregon Territory
Aleksandr Romanov becomes tsar of Russia
Frederick Cook of New Orleans, patents a cotton-bale metallic tie
Government Printing Office purchases 1st printing plant, Washington
US creates Dakota & Nevada Territories out of the Nebraska & Utah territories
British newspaper "Morning Chronicle" begins publishing
Conferate General Early's army is defeated at the battle of Waynesboro, Virginia, during American Civil War
Freedman's Bureau founded for Black Education, 1865
Second Taranaki War: The Volkner Incident in New Zealand.
1st US company to make sewing needles by machine incorporated in Connecticut
1st Reconstruction act passed by US Congress
Howard University is chartered by General Oliver O. Howard in Washington, D.C.
Reconstruction of the South begins with the First Reconstruction Act passed by Congress, and Black voter registration begins
US Congress abolishes peonage in New Mexico
US Congress creates the Department of Education
University of Illinois opens

National Association of Professional Baseball Players officially adopts the batter's box; decide any player betting on his own team will be expelled; any player betting on any other team to forfeit his pay
The Convention of Constantinople is signed, guaranteeing free maritime passage through the Suez Canal during war and peace
Kansas passes 1st US antitrust law
Norwegian speed skater Oskar Fredriksen sets inaugural 5,000m world record of 9:19.8 in Stockholm, Sweden
1st US federal railroad legislation passed; required safety features
US Congress renames the Office of Immigration as the Bureau of Immigration
President McKinley signs bill creating Mt Rainier National Park (5th in US)
Hawaii's first telegraph company opens
United States Congress passes the Platt amendment, limiting the autonomy of Cuba as a condition for the withdrawal of American troops
Stanley Cup, Montreal Arena, Westmount, Quebec: Montreal Wanderers and Ottawa HC tie, 5-5; Montreal disqualified for refusing to play second game in Ottawa
Georges Feydeaus' "La Puce à l'Oreille" premieres in Paris
Great Britain, France, Germany & Italy asks Serbia to set no territorial demands
British vice admiral Sackville Hamilton Carden begins bombardment of Dardanelles forts
Vladmir Jabotinsky forms a Jewish military force to fight in Palestine
Jones Act: Puerto Rico territory created, US citizenship granted
1st congress of Communist International opens at the Kremlin
WBAP-AM, Fort Worth Texas, begins broadcasting
WLW-AM in Cincinnati, Ohio, begins radio transmissions
Dutch Socialists demand drastic disarmament
Japan's House of Representatives recognizes male suffrage
Nationwide road numbering system & US shield marker adopted
SDAP-Second-Faction of parliament demands drastic disarmament
US Congress creates Court of Customs & Patent Appeals
1st US indoor glider flight, St Louis Terminal Building
Australian golf trick-shot artist Joe Kirkwood posts an incredible 83 using only his putter over 18 holes at Belleaire, Florida; round includes 6 pars and 1 birdie
Sanriku earthquake of 8.4 and tsunami hit Japan, country's most powerful earthquake in 180 years
Union Pacific tests light-weight high-speed passenger train, Omaha
Mexico nationalizes oil
Landslides & floods cause over 200 deaths (Los Angeles California)
Trials of Soviet leaders begins in the Soviet Union
Massachusetts Legislature votes to ratify the US Bill of Rights - 147 years late
Beaten by a nose in both the 1937 and 1938 Santa Anita Handicaps, Seabiscuit finally wins the Big 'Cap in his final race; retires the leading money-winning horse in the world
Soviet armies conquer Tuppura Island, Finland
The first televised intercollegiate track meet is seen in NYC on W2XBS; NYU wins the meet presented live from Madison Square Garden
World War II: First German military units enter Bulgaria after it joined the Axis Pact.
Admiral Helfrich departs Java for Ceylon
1st transport from Westerbork Netherlands to Sobibor concentration camp
Fumes from locomotive stalled in a tunnel suffocate 521 in Italy
King Michael of Romania gives in to Communist government
US 8th Air Force bombs Dresden
Dutch troops land on East Bali
Kingman Douglass becomes deputy director of CIA
1st automatic street light (New Milford, Ct)
Lucky Lady II (USAF B-50 Superfortress), completes 1st nonstop round- the-world flight at Fort Worth, Texas, covering 23,452-mis in 94 hrs
King Norodom Sihanukh of Cambodia succeeded by his father
William Inge's "Bus Stop" premieres in NYC
French-Moroccan Agreement signed in Paris rescinds the Treaty of Fez, declaring independence of Morocco from France
1st surface crossing of Antarctic continent is completed in 99 days
Yemen announces it would join the United Arab Republic
Montcalm Community College founded in Sidney, Mich
US Air Force begins Operation Rolling Thunder, a three year sustained bombing campaign against North Vietnam
215,000 US soldiers in Vietnam
After suffering a hairline fracture of his right hind foot, 5-time US Horse of the Year Kelso is immediately retired; racing's all-time leading money winner with lifetime earnings of $1,977,896
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Ice Dance Championship at Geneva won by Towler & Ford of Great Britain
Ice Pairs Championship at Geneva won by Belousova & Protopopov of the Soviet Union
Men's Figure Skating Champions in Geneva won by Emmerich Danzer of Austria
USAF displays Lockheed C-5A Galaxy, biggest plane in the world, with a cargo compartment 37m long
USSR launches space probe Zond 4; fails to leave Earth orbit
1st test flight of the supersonic Concorde
Chinese-Russian borders fight (approx 70 die)
American Airlines begins their first service using a Boeing 747, between New York's JFK International Airport and Los Angeles International
Supreme Court ruled draft evaders can not be penalized after 5 years
White government of Rhodesia declares itself a republic
NASA launches its Pioneer 10 space probe to Jupiter, NASA's first mission to the outer planets
"Black September" terrorists occupy Saudi Embassy in Khartoum
1st class postage raised to 10 cents from 8 cents
Walt Disney World logs its 50 millionth guest
Libya amends constitution
Soyuz 28 carries 2 cosmonauts (1 Czechoslovakian) to Salyut 6
Phoenix point guard Mike Bratz ends NBA free throw streak of 57 games with a miss in the Suns' 123-115 home win over the LA Lakers
Aircraft hijacked by 3 Pakistani terrorists
Howard Stern begins broadcasting on WWDC in Washington, D.C.
Terror group "The Illuminated Path" frees 260 prisoners in Peru
Compact Disc recordings developed by Phillips & Sony introduced
USSR performs underground nuclear test
Iran offensive against Iraq fails
FDA in the US approves an ELISA screening test for AIDS antibody for all blood banks
1st million-dollar purse for a handicap race won at Santa Anita
Protesters try to stop Land Rover motor company being sold to US
Qualifier Kenny Knox is the last player to score in the 80’s (R3 80) and still win a PGA Tour event, the Honda Classic at TPC Eagle Trace; total 287 is highest winning score in PGA tournament history
The flaming basketball is adopted as NBA franchise Miami Heat’s official logo from over 13,000 entries; Mark Henderson submits the winning entry
12 European nations agree to ban chlorofluorocarbon production by 2000

GreenLeft (GroenLinks) political party forms from merger of four left-wing parties: the Communist Party, the Pacifist Socialist Party, the Political Party of Radicals, and the Evangelical People's Party in the Netherlands
Tanker Exxon Houston runs aground in Hawaii, spills 117,000 gallons of oil
Greyhound Bus goes on strike
Del Ballard Jr throws most famous gutter ball in PBA Tour history
NC State point guard Chris Corchiani becomes 1st NCAA Div I player to record 1,000 career assists in a 89-84 loss to Wake Forest
UN votes in favor of US resolutions for cease fire with Iraq
US Army controversially destroys a retreating Iraqi Republican Guard column at Rumaila Oil Field, despite a ceasefire being observed
American swimmer Anita Nall breaks the 200m breaststroke world record twice in the one day at the US Swimming championships in Indianapolis; wins final in 2:25.35
Moldova joins the United Nations.
Branch Davidian cult leader David Koresh promises to surrender if taped statement is broadcasted, it is, but he doesn't
Winnipeg Jets Finnish right wing Teemu Selänne breaks NHL record for goals by a rookie with a hat-trick in a 7-4 loss to the visiting Quebec Nordiques; passes Mike Bossy's mark of 52
American politician William Natcher, (Rep-D-Ky), casts his 18,401 & last consecutive vote
"Smokey Joe's Cafe" opens at Virginia Theater NYC
British trader Nick Leeson arrested for collapse of Barings Bank PLC
Ferry boat sinks off Sumbe Angola, 42+ killed
Joe Mullen becomes 2nd NHL player to appear in 300 NHL games with 3 different teams when he plays for the Penguins in a 6-3 loss in Buffalo; Pittsburgh 379, St. Louis 301, Calgary 345
Space shuttle STS-67 (Endeavour 8), launches
Australians Greg Blewett (214) and Steve Waugh (160) bat for the entire 3rd day of the 1st cricket Test against South Africa in Johannesburg; set up Australian innings and 196 run victory
Michael Andretti wins at Homestead in a Swift chassis, marking the first Champ Car victory in an American-built chassis since 1983; first-ever Champ Car start for the Swift
Saudi Arab billionaire Prince al-Waleed bin Talal acquires 5% of Apple
Soyuz TM-24 returns to Earth (Russia)
Data sent from the Galileo spacecraft indicates that Jupiter's moon Europa has a liquid ocean under a thick crust of ice
St. Louis Blues become just the 2nd team in NHL history to win 10 straight games on the road, with a 5-2 victory in Atlanta; tie mark set by Buffalo 1983-84
40-year old South African boxer ‘Baby Jake’ Matlala ends his 22-year career with a 7th-round TKO win over Columbia’s Juan Herrera to retain his WBU junior flyweight title in Johannesburg
Portuguese explorerVasco da Gama's fleet visits Mozambique Island
English KingCharles Idissolves Parliament against opposition, imprisoning 9 members of parliament
PrinceWilliam IIIinstalled as governor of Overijssel
Nathanael Greeneis appointed Quartermaster General of the Continental Army underGeorge Washington
Alexandre de Beauharnais, first husband ofJoséphine de Beauharnais, is arrested during the Reign of Terror (later executed)
Napoléon Bonaparte is appointed Commander-in-Chief of the French Army in Italy
William Cavendish-Bentinck,Duke of Portlandbecomes British Prime Minister for the second time after the fall ofWilliam Grenville's Whig government
Republic of Texas declares independencefrom Mexico in Columbia
Jesse James' gang robs bank in Savannah Missouri, 1 killed
Louis Rielgoes into voluntary exile in St. Paul, Minnesota
US Electoral Commission declaresRutherford B. Hayes(R) winner of the presidential election with an electoral vote of 185-184 against Samuel J. Tilden (D)
Queen Victorianarrowly escapes assassination when Roderick Maclean shoots at her while she is boarding a train in Windsor
US Steel Corporation organized underJ. P. Morgan, Sr. through merger of Carnegie Steel Company, Federal Steel Company, and National Steel Company
Martha WashingtonHotel, catering to women only, opens in NYC
Gabriele d'Annunzio's tragic play "La figlia di Iorio" premieres in Milan
GeneralLouis Bothanamed premier of Transvaal
Joe Malonegoes scoreless in Montreal Canadiens' 5-3 season-ending loss to Toronto Arenas; finishes the first NHL season with 44 goals, a record that stands until 1944-45
Karel Čapek's play "Loupeznik" (Highwayman) premieres in Prague
George Hainsworthbecomes first NHL goaltender to record 20 shutouts in one season when the Montreal Canadiens beat the visiting Boston Bruins, 3-0; ends season with 22
Eugenio Pacelli chosen as PopePius XII
14th Academy Awards: "How Green was My Valley",Gary Cooper, andJoan Fontainewin
16th Academy Awards: "Casablanca",Jennifer Jones&Paul Lukaswin
Ho Chi Minhis elected President of North Vietnam
"Lawdy Miss Clawdy" single cover released byElvis Presley
Burmese army led by GeneralNe Winseizes power in a coup d'état, transforming Burma into a one-party socialist state under the Burmese Way to Socialism ideology

Philadelphia centerWilt Chamberlainscores 100 points, the most ever by an NBA player in a single game, in the Warriors' 169-147 win over the NY Knicks in Hershey; 36-of-63 from the field, 28-of-32 from the free-throw line
PresidentJohn F. Kennedyannounces US will resume above ground nuclear testing
Beatles begin filming "A Hard Day's Night",George Harrisonmeets future wifePattie Boyd
One of the most popular musical films of all time, "The Sound of Music," starringJulie AndrewsandChristopher Plummer, premieres (Academy Award for Best Picture - 1966)
Chicago Black Hawks right wingBobby Hullbecomes the NHL's first 2-time 50-goal scorer when he strikes in a 5-4 win against the Detroit Red Wings at Chicago Stadium
Jean Béliveauscores his 380th career NHL goal when Montreal ties Toronto, 3-3; moves him ahead of Ted Lindsay into the #3 spot all time, behind Gordie Howe and Maurice Richard
9th Grammy Awards: "Strangers in the Night" byFrank Sinatrawins Best Record, "Michele" by The Beatles wins Best Song
Boston centerPhil Espositobecomes first player in NHL history to record 100 points in a season when he scores 2 3rd-period goals in the Bruins' 4-0 win over the visiting Pittsburgh Penguins
Dmitri Shostakovichcompletes his 14th Symphony
Reprise Records releases "Ladies of the Canyon", Canadian singer-songwriterJoni Mitchell's 3rd studio album
American jockeyBill Shoemakerwins his record 555th stakes race aboard Royal Owl in the San Jacinto Stakes at Santa Anita;Eddie Arcaro554
16th Grammy Awards:Roberta FlackBest Record - "Killing Me Softly",Stevie WonderBest Album - "Innervisions"
Grand jury concludes US PresidentRichard Nixonis involved in Watergate cover-up
Bette Davisis 1st woman to receive American Film Institute's Life Achievement Award
Future The Tonight Show hostJay Lenodebuts with hostJohnny Carson
Discovery of minor planet 5020 Asimov, named after sci-fi writerIsaac Asimov
Edmonton's Finnish right wingJari Kurriscores 2 goals, including the overtime winner, to lead Oilers to a 2-1 win over Philadelphia; Kurri, 100 points for the 4th straight NHL season
The first million dollar purse for a handicap race is won by British bred 5-year old Greinton withLaffit Pincay Jr.aboard taking out the $1,359,500 Santa Anita Handicap
30th Grammy Awards: Record of the year -Paul Simon"Graceland"; Album of the Year -U2"The Joshua Tree"; Jody Watley Best New Artist
Madonna's "Like a Prayer" premieres on worldwide Pepsi commercial
NY Mets starsKeith HernandezandDarryl Strawberryfamously nearly come to blows in front of reporters and TV crews at spring training
St. Louis right wingBrett Hullbecomes a 70-goal scorer for the 2nd straight season and has 3 assists to help the Blues earn a 4-4 tie in Philadelphia; finishes season with NHL career-best 86 goals
ActressClaudette Colbertsuffers a stroke at 89
Mario Lemieuxundergoes his 22nd and final radiation treatment for Hodgkin lymphoma, then joins the Pittsburgh Penguins in Philadelphia and has a goal and assist in a 5-4 loss to the Flyers
Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,James Taylor,Bryan Adams,Tom Jones,George Michael, andHerb Alpert
Miami begins a Latin Walk of Fame, 1st star awarded is for singer-songwriterGloria Estefan
America's Cup, Auckland:Team New Zealandbeats Italian yacht Prada Challenge by 0.48s for a 5-0 series sweep; NZ only the 2nd country in 150 years to successfully defend the Cup
Former Chilean dictator GeneralAugusto Pinochetheads home after being told the UK would not extradite him on torture charges
"King Kong" filmdirected by Merian C. Cooper and Ernest B. Schoedsack, starring Fay Wray premieres at Radio City Music Hall and RKO Roxy in NYC
Ceremony to mark the start of construction of the Sydney Opera House at Bennelong Point in Sydney, Australia; estimates of  $7M and four years to complete project balloon to $102M and 14 years
Jean-Bédel Bokassaappoints himself President for Life of the Central African Republic
Benedict of Nursia, Christian saint and founder of Western Christian monasticism, born in Norcia, Umbria, Odoacer's Kingdom (d. 547)
King Robert II of Scotland, first monach of the House of Stewart (1371-90), born in Paisley Abbey, Renfrewshire, Scotland (d. 1390)
John II, French duke of Alencon (fought alongside Jeanne d'arc), born at Château d'Argentan, France (d. 1476)
Adrian VI [Adriaan F Boeyens], Dutch Pope (1522-23) last non-Italian pope for 455 years, born in Utrecht, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1523)
Franz von Sickingen, German knight, born in Bad Münster am Stein-Ebernburg, Palatine County of Lorraine, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1523)
Thomas Bodley, English diplomat and scholar (founder of the Bodleian Library), born in Exeter, England (d. 1612)
Ludovico Spontoni, Italian Renaissance composer, baptized in Bologna, Papal States (d. pre 1609)
George Sandys, English colonist and poet, born in Bishopsthorpe, England (d. 1644)
William Murray, 1st Earl of Mansfield, Scottish judge and politician, born in Scone Palace, Scotland (d. 1793)
Antoine-Frederic Gresnick, Belgian composer, born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1799)
Johann Christian Friedrich Haeffner, German-Swedish organist, composer, conductor, choirmaster, and educator (Studentsång), born in Oberschönau, Saxony (d. 1833)
Camille Desmoulins, French journalist, pamphleteer and revolutionary leader (his oratory led to storming of the Bastille), born in Guise, France (d. 1794)
DeWitt Clinton, American politician (Senator, Governor and Mayor of New York, had Erie Canal constructed), born in Little Britain, New York (d. 1828)
Louis-Gabriel Suchet, French nobleman (Duke of Albufera), and military commander (Marshal of the Empire), born in Lyon, France (d. 1826)
Joel Roberts Poinsett, American statesman (Minister to Mexico, 1825-29; Secretary of War, 19837-41), physician, and botanist, born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1851)
Evgeny Baratynsky, Russian poet, born in Vyazhlya, Russia (d. 1844)
George Alexander Macfarren, British composer and musicologist, born in London, England (d. 1887)
Antonio Buzzolla, Italian composer, born in Adria, Napoleonic Kingdom of Italy (d. 1871)
Alexander Bullock, American lawyer, politician, and 26th Governor of Massachusetts, born in Royalston, Massachusetts (d. 1882)
Janos Arany, Hungarian epic poet (Toldi, Death of King Buda), born in Nagyszalonta, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1882)
Multatuli [Eduard Douwes Dekker], Dutch writer (Max Havelaar), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1887)
Henry Beebee Carrington, American lawyer, professor, prolific author, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Wallingford, Connecticut (d. 1912)
Konstantin Ushinsky, Russian educationalist (credited as the founder of scientific pedagogy in Russia), born in Tula, Russia (d. 1870)
Jefferson Columbus Davis, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Clark County, Indiana (d. 1879)
Carl Schurz, German-American journalist and Major General (Union Army), born in Erftstadt, Germany (d. 1906)
Henry Billings Brown, American jurist (U.S. Supreme Court Justice, 1891-1906), born in Lee, Massachusetts (d. 1913)
David Duffle Wood, American blind composer, organist, and choir master (St. Stephen's Episcopal Church), born near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1910)
Carl Jacobsen, Danish brewer (Carlsburg), and philanthropist, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1914)
Maria Clotilde of Savoy, Princess Napoléon, wife of Prince Napoléon-Jérôme Bonaparte, born in the Royal Palace of Turin, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1911)
Herman Schaepman 'the Doctor', Dutch poet, Roman Catholic priest, theologian, and politician, born in Tubbergen, Netherlands (d. 1903)
Robert Means Thompson, American US Navy officer, lawyer, business magnate, and administrator (American Olympic Association), born in Corsica, Pennsylvania (d. 1930)
Sholem Aleichem [Solomon Rabinowitz], Yiddish author and playwright (Fiddler on the Roof, Tevye the Dairyman), born in Pereyaslav, Russian Empire (d. 1916)
Susanna M. Salter, 1st American female mayor and temperance leader, born in Lamira, Ohio (d. 1961)
John Jay Chapman, American poet, and writer (Learning & Other Essays), born in New York City (d. 1933)
Théo Ysaÿe, Belgian composer, and pianist, born in Verviers, Belgium (d. 1918)
Hans Lietzman, German theologist and church historian, born in Düsseldorf (d. 1942)
Wander Johannes de Haas, Dutch physicist (Shubnikov–de Haas effect), born in Lisse, Netherlands (d. 1960)
Wallis Clark, British-American stage and screen actor (Penny Serenade; Great Guy; Postal Inspector; Easy Money), born in Essex, England (d. 1961)
Léon Jongen, Belgian organist, composer, and academic, born in Liège, Belgium (d. 1969)
(Willis) "Obie" O'Brien, American Academy Award-winning stop-action animator (The Lost World; King Kong; Mighty Joe Young), born in Oakland, California (d. 1962)
Oscar Egg, Swiss track and road bicycle racer (3 x world hour record), born in Schlatt, Switzerland (d. 1961)
Felix Bressart, German actor (Ninotchka, Escape, Crossroads), born in Eydtkuhnen, East Prussia, Germany (d. 1949)
Maxime Dumoulin, French composer, born in Lille, France (d. 1972)
Renaat Veremans, Flemish organist, composer, and conductor (Flemish Opera, Antwerp, 1921-44), born in Lierre, Belgium (d. 1969)

Clair Bee, American College Basketball and Basketball Hall of Fame coach (Long Island University undefeated 1936, 39; National Invitation Tournament 1939, 41), born in Grafton, West Virginia (d. 1983)
M. Lincoln Schuster, Austrian-born American book publisher and co-founder of Simon & Schuster, born in Kałusz, Austria-Hungry (d. 1970)
Harald Søltoft Agersnap, Danish cellist, pianist, conductor, and composer (Royal Danish Theatre, 1931-66), born in Vinding, Denmark (d. 1982)
Pattie Menzies, Australian wife of PM Robert Menzies, born in Alexandra, Australia (d. 1995)
Kurt Weill, German-American composer and collaborator of Bertolt Brecht (Die Dreigroschenoper - The Threepenny Opera), born in Dessau, Germany (d. 1950)
Edward Condon, American nuclear physicist (pioneer in quantum mechanics, involved in Manhattan Project), born in Alamogordo, New Mexico (d. 1974)
Marc Blitzstein, American opera and theater composer, librettist (The Cradle Will Rock), and translator (Bertolt Brecht and Kurt Weill works), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1964)
Jheri [Robert] Redding, American hairdresser and businessman (created the Jheri curl and hair conditioner), born in Rantoul, Illinois (d. 1998)
Fyodor Okhlopkov, Yakut-born Soviet sniper considered one of the deadliest snipers in history, born in Krest-Khal'dzhai, Russian Empire (d. 1968)
Jan Brasser, Dutch resistance fighter known as Witte Ko, and communist, born in Uitgeest, Netherlands (d. 1991)
Walter Bruch, German electrical engineer, and television equipment pioneer, born in Neustadt an der Weinstraße, German Empire (d. 1990)
Hanoch Jacoby, Israeli violist (Israel Philharmonic, 1958-74), educator, and composer, born in Königsberg, German Empire (d. 1990)
William Hansen, American actor (1776, Pinky, Side Street, Homebodies), born in Washington, District of Columbia (d. 1975)
(Theodore) "Red" Saunders, American jazz drummer and bandleader, based in Chicago, born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1981)
Arthur Langton, South African cricket medium pace bowler (15 Tests, 40 wickets), born in Pietermaritzburg, Natal, South Africa (d. 1942)
Celedonio Romero, Spanish-American guitarist (The Romero Guitar Quartet), composer (La Catedral de Colonia), and poet, born in Cienfuegos, Cuba (d. 1996)
Georgy Flyorov, Soviet-Russian physicist (spontaneous fission, Soviet atomic bomb project), born in Rostov-on-Don, Russian Empire (d. 1990)
Godfried Bomans, Dutch humorist and writer and TV personality (Pieter Bas), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1971)
Marjorie Weaver, American actress (Michael Shayne: Private Detective, Young Mr Lincoln), born in Crossville, Tennessee (d. 1994)
Mort Cooper, American baseball pitcher (MLB All-Star 1942, 43, 45, 46; World Series 1942, 44; NL MVP 1942), born in Atherton, Missouri (d. 1958)
Lona Andre [Launa Anderson], American actress (Death in the Air, Case of Baby Sitter), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1992)
Bernard George Stevens, British composer, born in London, England (d. 1983)
Alex Graham, Scottish cartoonist who created the comic strip "Fred Basset", born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1991)
David Goodis, American crime fiction writer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1967)
Jim Konstanty, American MLB relief pitcher (MLB All Star 1950; NL MVP 1950; Philadelphia Phillies), born in Strykersville, New York (d. 1976)
John Gardner, English classical composer, born in Manchester, England (d. 2011)
Eddie Lawrence, American comedian and monologist (The Old Philosopher), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2014)[1]
Tamara Toumanova, Georgian-American prima ballerina (Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo), born in Tyumen, Russian (d. 1996)
Enrique Franco, Spanish pianist, composer (Cuerda de presos), music promoter, and broadcaster (National Spanish Radio; Radio Clásica), born in Madrid Spain (d. 2009)
Julia Parsons, American naval codebreaker and member of the Women Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2025)
Norman T. Hatch, American oscar-winning marine cinematographer during WWII (With the Marines at Tarawa), born in Boston (d. 2017)
Robert Simpson, British classical composer, and broadcaster (BBC), born in Leamington, Warwickshire, England (d. 1997)
Wilhelm Büsing, German equestrian (Olympic silver team eventing, bronze Individual eventing 1952) and coach (Olympics 1956, 60, 64), born in Jade, Germany (d. 2023)
Bill Quackenbush, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame defenceman (8 x NHL All-Star; Boston Bruins, Detroit Red Wings) and coach (Princeton), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1999)
Eddie "Lockjaw" Davis, American jazz saxophonist (Count Basie; Kenny Clarke/Francy Boland Big Band), and bandleader, born in Culver City, California (d. 1986)
Eric Feldbush [Désiré Feldbusch], Belgian cellist, conductor, and composer (Pastels), born in Grivegnée, Belgium (d. 2007)
Mario Zafred, Italian composer, music critic, and opera director, born in Trieste, Italy (d. 1987)

György Pásztor, Hungarian ice hockey forward (6 x Hungarian C'hip; Budapest SC, Red Meteor SC; Hungarian IHHOF) and administrator (est. Hungarian IH Federation), born in Törökbálint, Hungary (d. 2022)
Orrin Keepnews, American jazz producer and founder of Riverside Records and Milestone Records labels, born in The Bronx, New York City (d. 2015)
Robert H. Michel, American politician (Rep-R-IL, 1957-95), born in Peoria, Illinois (d. 2017)
Alexander "Papa" Lightfoot, American blues singer and harmonica player, born in Natchez, Mississippi (d. 1971)
Maxwell Street Jimmy Davis [Charles Thompson], American blues musician (Cold Hands), born in Tippo, Mississippi (d. 1995)
Murray Rothbard, American economist, and libertarian, born in The Bronx, NYC (d. 1995)
John Romanides, Greek Eastern Orthodox priest and professor, born in Piraeus, Greece (d. 2001)
Ray Prosser, Welsh rugby union prop (22 caps Wales, 1 British & Irish Lions; Pontypool) and coach (Pontypool RFC 1969-87), born in Pontypool, Wales (d. 2020)
Roger Walkowiak, French road cyclist (Tour de France 1956), born in Montluçon, France (d. 2017)
Siegfried Köhler, German composer (Tausend Sterne sind ein Dom), born in Meissen, Germany (d. 1984)
Witold Szalonek, Polish composer (Satire), educator, and musicologist, born in Czechowice-Dziedzice, Poland (d. 2001)[1]
John Manduell, British-based South African composer and educator (Royal Northern College of Music in Manchester, 1973-96), born in Johannesburg, Union of South Africa (d. 2017)
José Luis Pérez-Payá, Spanish soccer forward (2 caps; Atlético Madrid, Real Madrid), born in Alcoy, Spain (d. 2022)
Sir Donald Gosling, British entrepreneur (Central Car Parks; National Car Parks), yachtsman, and WWII Royal Navy veteran, born in South London, England (d. 2019)
Emma Penella, Spanish actress, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 2007)
John Cullum, American Tony Award-winning stage and screen singer and actor (Hamlet; 1776; Hawaii; Northern Exposure - "Holling"; The Middle - "Big Mike"), born in Knoxville, Tennessee
Duane Graveline, doctor and astronaut, born in Newport Vermont (d. 2016)
Tom Wolfe, American journalist and author (The Right Stuff), born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 2018)
Frank E. Petersen, American soldier and both the 1st African American Marine Corps aviator and Marine Corps general, born in Topeka Kansas (d. 2015)
Bernard Rands, British-American composer (Wildtrack), born in Sheffield, England
Dottie Rambo [Joyce Luttrell], American gospel singer and songwriter, born in Madisonville, Kentucky (d. 2008)
